mnemonics.title: Don Norman's Three Levels
mnemonics.content: VBR - Visceral (appearance), Behavioral (use), Reflective (memory). Remember: Viscera=Instant, Behavior=Use, Reflection=Remembering back to the experience.

mnemonics.title: SAM Model Dimensions
mnemonics.content: VAD - Valence (Pleasantness), Arousal (Excitement), Dominance (Control). Remember: VAD sounds like 'Vivid And Dynamic' - emotions are vivid and dynamic!

mnemonics.title: Emotional Design Goals
mnemonics.content: PEARL - Pleasure, Engagement, Attachment, Retention, Loyalty. Remember: Emotional design creates a PEARL of value for businesses.

acronyms.usage: The overall experience of a user when interacting with a product or service
Study of how humans interact with computers and the design of interfaces
Measurable benefits relative to the cost of emotional design implementation
A pictorial instrument for measuring emotional responses on valence, arousal, and dominance
The visual and interactive elements through which users interact with a product

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a traffic light: Red (Visceral-instant), Yellow (Behavioral-during use), Green (Reflective-after use). Just as traffic lights guide you through a journey, these levels guide the emotional journey.
Picture a 3D cube: X-axis = Valence (sad to happy), Y-axis = Arousal (calm to excited), Z-axis = Dominance (controlled to in control). Every emotion can be mapped within this 3D space.
Draw a line graph with User Journey stages on X-axis and Emotional State (negative to positive) on Y-axis. The peaks show delightful moments, valleys show pain points.
Picture a beautiful sports car (aesthetic) vs. a plain family car. We assume the sports car is faster and better, even if the family car has better practical features.

analogies.analogy: Like a skilled waiter who not only brings correct food but also remembers your preferences, makes conversation, and creates a pleasant atmosphere - the meal (function) is only part of the experience.
Like falling in love at first sight - immediate, instinctive attraction before you even know the person's personality (functionality).
Like a friendly nod from a colleague when you enter a room - small but makes you feel acknowledged and valued.
Like planting a tree: the initial effort (investment) may seem slow, but over time it grows value (loyal users, brand advocates) that compounds.
Like judging a book by its cover - we assume beautiful things work better, even though the cover doesn't affect the story quality.
